original_title: NIST Limits CVE Enrichment After 263% Surge in Vulnerability Submissions
selection_reasoning: This represents a fascinating systemic story about how success can become its own problem. The 263% surge shows how security awareness has grown, but now the very system meant to track vulnerabilities is overwhelmed. It's a story about scalability, priorities, and unintended consequences that translates well to fantasy metaphors.

field_note.title: The Overwhelmed Chroniclers
field_note.subtitle: When Success in Vigilance Becomes Its Own Burden
field_note.excerpt: The great library of vulnerabilities grows faster than the scribes can catalog, forcing difficult choices about what threats deserve attention.
field_note.epigraph: Sometimes the greatest challenge is not finding threats, but choosing which ones matter most.
body_paragraphs: In the great Archive of Known Weaknesses, the chroniclers of NIST had long maintained careful records of every crack, flaw, and vulnerability discovered across all realms. For years, their scribes diligently catalogued each submission, adding context, severity scores, and detailed analysis to help kingdoms understand which threats required immediate attention. This National Vulnerability Database had become the authoritative tome that defenders everywhere consulted when fortifying their domains.

But success brought unexpected consequences. As awareness of security grew across the lands, more and more observers began reporting weaknesses they discovered. Submissions surged by 263%-a flood of vulnerabilities that arrived faster than the chroniclers could properly examine and categorize. Where once dozens of flaws might be reported in a season, now hundreds arrived each week, each demanding careful analysis to determine its true danger.

The Archive's scribes found themselves drowning in their own success. Every reported weakness deserved attention, yet the chroniclers possessed neither infinite time nor unlimited resources. Like librarians watching their collection grow beyond any hope of proper organization, they faced an impossible choice: provide incomplete records for all vulnerabilities, or complete analysis for only a select few.

In their wisdom, the chroniclers chose triage over completeness. They announced that only the most critical vulnerabilities-those affecting widely-used systems or posing immediate dangers-would receive their full attention and enrichment. Lesser flaws would still be recorded in the great ledgers, but without the detailed analysis that had once been standard. It was a sobering reminder that even in the realm of security, infinite vigilance must bow to the reality of finite resources.
